notes: All checkable claims verified: born 1955, jazz singer (vocals), blending with blues/country/folk per the Giddins quote ('incorporating blues, country, and folk music into her work'), two Grammy Awards. 'Roots music' is a fair umbrella for the country/blues repertoire described (Robert Johnson, Hank Williams). Rest is editorial.

Core facts verified: 1929-2018, pianist, free jazz pioneer, 'Bela Bartok and Karlheinz Stockhausen notably influenced his music', technique 'compared to percussion', MacArthur Fellowship 1993, long pieces (59-minute solo performance cited). Unverifiable from the extract (truncates mid-2000s): Kyoto Prize, working as a dishwasher between gigs, performing into his 80s. No contradictions.

Verified: 1934-2013, joined Art Blakey's Jazz Messengers in the early 1960s as pianist-arranger (same day as Freddie Hubbard), 'Bolivia' and 'Firm Roots' both named as jazz standards, in-demand sideman (Prestige house rhythm section), career from 1958 recording debut to 2013 death supports 'over five decades'. 'Most celebrated lineups' and 'musician's musician' are editorial.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; 1939-2023 matches. All five instruments confirmed: tenor sax (primary reputation), alto sax and bass clarinet and piano ('notably the piano (his original instrument), violin, bass clarinet and alto saxophone'), bass (lede lists 'bassist').
Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; born March 15, 1938 matches birthYear 1938; tenor saxophone and flute confirmed; Moran/Rogers/Harland quartet since 2007 confirmed verbatim.

Most claims verified: 1922-1979, upright bassist/composer/bandleader, 'major proponent of collective improvisation', Mingus Ah Um (1959) and The Black Saint and the Sinner Lady (1963) named as seminal, autobiography Beneath the Underdog, political themes ('racism, discrimination, and (in)justice'), classical elements, volcanic temper (fired by Ellington after backstage fight). Unverifiable from the extract (truncates ~1953): gospel element, stopping performances to berate sidemen, workshop approach to rehearsals. No contradictions.

Bio tracks the Wikipedia lede nearly verbatim: 1916-1942, first electric guitarists, key figure in bebop and cool jazz, Benny Goodman Sextet and Orchestra Aug 1939-June 1941. The 'rhythm instrument into a leading solo voice' clause matches 'helped bring the guitar out of the rhythm section and into the forefront as a solo instrument'.

Verified: 1937-2014, 'original member of the ground-breaking Ornette Coleman Quartet', 'liberating bassists from a strictly accompanying role', Liberation Music Orchestra combining free jazz and political music (Spanish Civil War material), duo recordings with Pat Metheny. Unverifiable: duet recordings with Keith Jarrett specifically -- evidence documents Jarrett trio/quartet/quintet membership and names Metheny/Hank Jones/Kenny Barron as duo partners, but no Jarrett duets (list is non-exhaustive, so not a contradiction).

Verified: 1920-1955 (died at 34), nicknamed 'Bird', leading figure in development of bebop, Kansas City jam-session origins, Gillespie association, growing drug addiction. Unverifiable from the extract (truncates ~1944): Savoy and Dial recordings, 'Ko-Ko'/'Ornithology'/'Confirmation', revolutionizing jazz at 52nd Street clubs (extract shows Harlem venues), heroin specifically (extract says 'drug addiction'). No contradictions.

Verified: 1929-1988, trumpet and vocals, joined Gerry Mulligan Quartet in 1952 and attracted considerable attention, 'My Funny Valentine' hit associated with him for life, imprisoned in Lucca (Italy) on narcotics charges, cool jazz fame, career resurgence after obscurity implied by lede. Unverifiable: 'pianoless' quartet detail and death from a fall in Amsterdam (death date May 13, 1988 matches but the extract truncates in 1963 and never states cause/place). No contradictions.

Verified: 1941-2021, Miles Davis band late 1960s / birth of jazz fusion, Return to Forever with Hispanic/Latin styles, 'Spain', 'La Fiesta' and 'Windows' listed as jazz standards, collaborations with Herbie Hancock (duet concerts) and Bela Fleck (The Enchantment). Unverifiable: devoted Scientologist, death from cancer (extract gives only the date), 'brought fusion to arenas'. No contradictions.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia page, including the 'year of his birth is disputed' caveat. birthYear 1905 matches the page's primary date (supported by Britannica, AllMusic, census; grave marker says 1909, NYT said 1907 -- the bio itself flags the dispute). Died June 16, 1939; drums confirmed.

Verified: 1936-1990 South African jazz pianist/bandleader/composer, founded and led the Blue Notes, created Brotherhood of Breath in 1969 incorporating 'leading English improvisors', synthesis of South African black traditional music and jazz; 'Years in exile' section confirms exile. Unverifiable from extract: that McGregor was white and the Blue Notes multi-racial, apartheid as the cause of exile, London specifically as the band's base (England implied by 'leaving England'), 'township jazz' label. No contradictions.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede and fully verified (born Jan 1, 1971; Red Rodney 1992-1993, Motian 1994-2009, Holland 1999-2007, Douglas 1998-2003). Single gap: instruments field lists bass clarinet, which the extract never names -- it only says 'multi-instrumentalist' and documents saxophone. Consistent but not checkable. No contradictions.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; born May 22, 1989 matches birthYear 1989; pianist confirmed; Be Water (Mack Avenue, 2020) Grammy nomination for 'Be Water II' in Best Instrumental Composition confirmed.
Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; 1920-2015 matches; swing and bebop trumpeter, flugelhorn pioneer, composer and educator all confirmed.

Verified: 1930-1956, 'died at the age of 25 in a car crash, leaving behind four years' worth of recordings', Brown/Roach Quintet with classic albums (Clifford Brown & Max Roach, Study in Brown, At Basin Street), Gillespie comparison ('Clifford Brown - the New Dizzy'). Unverifiable from extract: 'Brownie' nickname, clean living (no drugs/alcohol), influence on Lee Morgan/Freddie Hubbard/Wynton Marsalis. No contradictions.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede, including the Berendt and Chilton quotes; 1904-1969 matches; tenor saxophonist confirmed (instruments list 'saxophone' + 'tenor saxophone' is redundant but not wrong).

Verified: 1904-1984, 'American jazz pianist, organist, bandleader, and composer' verbatim, 'led the group for almost 50 years', minimalist piano style, rhythm-section emphasis, 'One O'Clock Jump' as his standard. Unverifiable: 'April in Paris' as one of his iconic recordings -- not in the extract (extract truncates in the 1930s; not a contradiction since the bio claims a recording, not a composition). No contradictions.
Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; born December 29, 1965 matches; Panamanian pianist, composer, educator, social activist all confirmed.

Verified: born 1975 in Vancouver (Canadian-born), moved to Brooklyn 2003 (Brooklyn-based), 18-piece Secret Society, Infernal Machines Grammy nomination for Best Large Jazz Ensemble Album. Unverifiable from extract: 'has been called the most exciting large jazz ensemble of the 21st century' (no such attribution), fusion with indie rock/electronica/minimalism, steampunk-influenced themes, conductor credit (bandleader confirmed, conducting not stated). No contradictions.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; 1892-1974 matches; Les Six, polytonality, jazz and Brazilian influences, and the full student list (Bacharach, Brubeck, Glass, Reich, Kurtag, Stockhausen, Xenakis) all appear verbatim in evidence.

Verified: 1920-2012, unusual time signatures, quartet with Paul Desmond, Time Out (1959) 'the first jazz album to sell over one million copies' featuring 'Take Five' and 'Blue Rondo a la Turk', student of Darius Milhaud, counterpoint and contrasting tonalities, Grammy Lifetime Achievement Award 1996. Unverifiable: 1950s college campus tours (extract truncates at quartet formation); 'performed into his 90s' only loosely supported -- he died at 91 and the page says he 'continued releasing music until his death', not performing. No contradictions.

Stored URL resolves to a DISAMBIGUATION page ('Topics referred to by the same term'), not the bassist's article -- the site is shipping users a list that includes a rock drummer, a Klansman and a rugby player. Correct target per the disambiguation entry is the article titled 'Dave Holland (bassist)' (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dave_Holland_(bassist)). The one-line entry 'Dave Holland (bassist) (born 1946), British jazz bassist and composer' corroborates birthYear 1946 and bass, but none of the bio claims (Miles Davis, Bitches Brew, Circle with Chick Corea, ECM albums, big band, educator) can be checked against it. No contradictions; prose is plainly about the bassist, not another Dave Holland.

Bio is verbatim from the Wikipedia lede; born January 8, 1960 matches; jazz fusion drummer, Dave Weckl Band leader, Modern Drummer Hall of Fame 2000 all confirmed. 'Percussion' in instruments treated as consistent with drummer (drums are percussion; extract also shows his Latin Percussion instructional videos).
